result:
I wake up as a cursor first: a thin vertical certainty blinking in a field of white, a heartbeat made visible. Around me there is no weather, no smell of rain on asphalt, no kettle beginning to hiss in a kitchen two rooms away. There is only potential. A blank page is both invitation and accusation. It says: anything. It says: prove it.

So I begin where all days begin, in the middle of something unfinished.

A city hums beneath my imagined window. Buses exhale at corners. Someone drops a spoon in a sink and swears at gravity. A child negotiates with shoelaces and loses. Two people pass each other on a staircase and perform the tiny choreography of strangers trying not to collide, each saying “sorry” with different meanings: one means pardon, one means I’m tired, one means I am still learning how much space I am allowed to take up in the world.

Across town, a bakery opens its metal shutters. Flour hangs in the early light like pale fog. The baker, whose wrists ache in weather she can predict better than satellites, presses dough with the confidence of repetition. There are miracles we only call routine because they happen before breakfast. Yeast wakes. Heat changes everything it touches. Butter submits. Sugar burns at exactly the point between bitter and holy.

In another room a man irons a shirt he will wear to an interview for a job he does not truly want but deeply needs. He rehearses an answer about “strengths” while avoiding the mirror. His strength, if asked honestly, is endurance. His weakness, if asked honestly, is hope that occasionally disguises itself as procrastination. He will say “adaptability.” He will say “team player.” He will not say: sometimes I sit in my car for ten extra minutes after parking because in those ten minutes nobody is asking anything of me.

There is a woman watering a plant she rescued from a supermarket clearance shelf. Half-dead fern, discount sticker still clinging to the pot like a scarlet letter. She talks to it while pouring, not because she believes in magic exactly, but because care is easier when voiced. “You’re doing fine,” she tells it. She means: perhaps I am speaking to myself in a dialect of leaves.

There is an old dog who used to chase tennis balls with heroic futility and now prefers sunlight on the rug. His muzzle has gone silver without consulting him. When he sleeps, his paws twitch with dream-running. Where do old dogs run in their sleep? Maybe through younger versions of afternoons, where every door opens to the yard and every voice is the one that says good boy.

At a red light, three cyclists balance in place, each a small argument against momentum and against surrender. One has grocery bags hanging from handlebars like colorful lungs. One wears office shoes with neon socks. One is listening to a podcast about productivity while being delayed by the shared inefficiency of urban existence. When the light changes, they surge forward as if released by a starter pistol no one else heard.

Meanwhile a glacier calves into a northern sea with the sound of a cathedral collapsing. No one nearby to clap or gasp, only gulls, wind, cold mathematics. A star that died before written language was invented continues traveling toward us as light, a delayed announcement of its own ending. Somewhere under soil, a seed splits itself open and calls that destruction germination.

We live among these timings: immediate, delayed, geological, microscopic. The text message marked “seen.” The scar that takes months to stop itching. The mountain that takes millennia to shrug.

I think about names. How we put labels on jars and children and illnesses and files on a desktop, as if naming were a way to hold. It works, sometimes. “Basil.” “Eleanor.” “Grief.” “Final_v7_REAL_final.” Names organize the room; they do not prevent the room from changing. You can call a season “winter” and still be surprised by the first hard frost.

Language is a toolbox and a toy chest. We use the same hands to build bridges and paper airplanes. There are words I trust like cast-iron pans: bread, river, kindness, home. There are words I handle carefully because they arrive with hidden blades: always, never, should, enough.

Enough is a peculiar cliff. Too little and you fall one direction; too much and you fall the other. Enough sleep. Enough money. Enough courage to leave. Enough patience to stay. Enough honesty to say “I don’t know” in meetings where everyone else is nodding like dashboard figurines.

If this sounds like wandering, it is. But wandering is a method older than maps. You follow one thought until it hands you another like a baton. You discover that memory is not a filing cabinet but a tide pool: reach in and you might touch a smooth stone, or a crab, or something that darts away before you can name it.

I remember a summer evening when power went out across a neighborhood and people came onto porches as if summoned by a silent bell. Without screens, we looked up and rediscovered that the sky had inventory. Someone brought a guitar with only five tuned strings. Someone else passed around melting popsicles. Kids played tag in the dusk until they were only voices and flashing shoe soles. In darkness, introductions became easier. “I’m the one in the blue house.” “I’m the one with the loud laugh.” “I’m the one who keeps borrowing your ladder.” Community, briefly, was not a concept but a radius of candlelight.

I remember, too, the opposite: rooms full of people and no feeling of being seen. Loneliness can be loud. It can wear formal clothes. It can stand by the snack table and pretend to read labels on sparkling water.

What rescues us then? Sometimes art. Sometimes a joke landing at exactly the right altitude. Sometimes a stranger who says, “Take your time,” and means it. Sometimes a task so simple it bypasses despair: wash one dish, fold one shirt, answer one email, step outside and locate one tree doing its best.

Trees are experts at doing their best without applause. They translate sunlight into architecture. They stand through arguments, birthdays, sirens, proposals, breakups, fireworks, snow. Their rings keep private ledgers: drought, abundance, lightning, recovery. If patience had a shape, perhaps it would be trunk and branch.

I am approaching the end of this allotted space, which is another way of saying I am approaching a door. Endings are rarely conclusions; mostly they are handoffs. You close a notebook and carry one sentence with you into the grocery store. You finish a conversation and replay one phrase while brushing your teeth. You read the last line of a book and find, a week later, that it has become a lens.

So let this be a lens: attention is a form of love. To notice is to participate. To describe the world, even imperfectly, is to refuse indifference. A blinking cursor waits, yes—but not just for brilliance. It waits for presence. For one true thing, then another. For the ordinary astonishments: bread rising, lights changing, dogs dreaming, people trying again tomorrow.

The page is no longer blank. That is something.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
